1380	
1381	## Array Safety Under `set -u`
1382	
1383	Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2. Two patterns are safe; choose by whether you need the index.
1384	
1385	**Index iteration** — use when you need the index (parallel arrays, kindle rolls). Inherently safe on empty arrays, no guard needed:
1386	
1387	```bash
1388	for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1389	  echo "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1390	done
1391	```
1392	
1393	**Guarded value iteration** — use when you only need values. The `(( ))` guard prevents expansion of the empty array:
1394	
1395	```bash
1396	if (( ${#z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]} )); then
1397	  for z_val in "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1398	    echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1399	  done
1400	fi
1401	```
1402	
1403	**Anti-pattern** — unguarded value iteration on a possibly-empty array:
1404	
1405	```bash
1406	# ❌ Crashes under set -u in bash 3.2 when array is empty
1407	for z_val in "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1408	  echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1409	done
1410	```

1413	
1414	## Stdin Consumption in While-Read Loops
1415	
1416	### The Problem
1417	
1418	When `while read ... done < file` feeds a file to stdin (FD 0), **any child process that reads stdin silently consumes the loop's remaining input**. This includes `docker exec -i`, `ssh`, `read` without explicit FD, `cat` without arguments, and any command that inherits stdin.
1419	
1420	The failure is **silent and partial**: the loop processes some iterations correctly, then stops early with no error. This is one of the hardest bash bugs to diagnose because the loop appears to work — it just processes fewer items than expected.
1421	
1422	### ❌ Anti-Pattern: Open-FD Loop Body
1423	
1424	```bash
1425	# ❌ File held open on stdin for entire loop — child processes can consume it
1426	while IFS= read -r z_item || test -n "${z_item}"; do
1427	    complex_function "${z_item}"    # If this touches stdin, remaining items vanish
1428	done < "${z_temp_file}"
1429	```
1430	
1431	### ✅ Pattern: Load-Then-Iterate
1432	
1433	**Always** read file contents into an array first, then iterate. The file is fully consumed and closed before any loop body code runs.
1434	
1435	```bash
1436	# ✅ Load phase — file consumed and closed
1437	local z_items=()
1438	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
1439	    z_items+=("${z_line}")
1440	done < "${z_temp_file}"
1441	rm -f "${z_temp_file}"
1442	
1443	# ✅ Iterate phase — stdin is free, no FD held open
1444	local z_i
1445	for z_i in "${!z_items[@]}"; do
1446	    test -n "${z_items[$z_i]}" || continue
1447	    complex_function "${z_items[$z_i]}"
1448	done
1449	```

1453	## Sourcing Rules
1454	
1455	Sourcing is restricted because it breaks error handling. Only three locations may source files:
1456	
1457	| Location             | Can Source                 | Purpose                                              |
1458	|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
1459	| CLI file header      | `buc_command.sh` only      | Bootstrap command infrastructure                     |
1460	| CLI Furnish Function | All deps + config files    | Module loading, environment setup                    |
1461	| Internal functions   | Credentials when necessary | Context-specific secrets (with documented rationale) |
1462	
1463	The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh` (via `BURD_BUK_DIR`). All other module and config sourcing happens inside the furnish function, after the `buc_doc_env` / `buc_doc_env_done` gate. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime dependencies.
1464	
1465	## Eval Policy
1466	
1467	`eval` is **forbidden** except for validated variable-name dereference. Require `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$` regex validation before any eval.
1468	
1469	**Prefer indirect expansion for reading** — `${!name}` works in bash 3.2:
1470	
1471	```bash
1472	# ✅ Indirect expansion for reading
1473	local z_val="${!z_varname}"
1474	```
1475	
1476	**Use `printf -v` for assignment to indirect variable** — after name validation:
1477	
1478	```bash
1479	# ✅ printf -v for assignment — no quoting hazards from values
1480	echo "${z_varname}" | grep -qE '^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$' \
1481	  || buc_die "Invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"
1482	printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_new_value}" || buc_die "printf -v failed"
1483	```
1484	
1485	**Anti-patterns:**
1486	
1487	```bash
1488	# ❌ eval for assignment — quoting hazards from values with quotes/newlines/backslashes
1489	eval "${z_varname}=\${z_new_value}"
1490	
1491	# ❌ Unvalidated eval — injection risk
1492	eval "local z_val=\${${z_varname}:-}"
1493	```
1494	
1495	## Command Dependency Discipline
1496	
1497	External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.
1498	
1499	### POSIX Utility Allowlist
1500	
1501	These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1502	
1503	`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`
1504	
1505	Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.
1506	
1507	### Evicted Utilities
1508	
1509	These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.
1510	
1511	| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
1512	|---------|-------------|-------|
1513	| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
1514	| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
1515	| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
1516	| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
1517	| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
1518	| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
1519	| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
1520	| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
1521	| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |
1522	
1523	### Declared Dependency Principle
1524	
1525	Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.
1526	
1527	The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.
1528	
1529	### Platform-Variant Command Guidance
1530	
1531	Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:
1532	
1533	- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
1534	- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
1535	- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid
1536	
1537	**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.
1538	
1539	### LLM Contributor Checkpoint
1540	
1541	LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.
1542	
1543	The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"
1544

1546	
1547	## Output and Messaging Patterns
1548	
1549	### Message Hierarchy
1550	
1551	buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
1552	buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
1553	command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
1554	buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr
1555	buc_die "Fatal error message"               # Red, stderr, exits
1556	
1557	### When to use buc_step vs buc_log_«source»:
1558	
1559	**Use buc_step in:**
1560	- Public command functions (rbv_start, rbv_stop)
1561	- Top-level user-initiated operations
1562	- Major state transitions visible to user
1563	
1564	**Use buc_log_«source» in:**
1565	- Internal helper functions (zrbgo_*)
1566	- Capture functions (*_capture) - keeps stdout clean
1567	- Predicate functions (*_predicate) - no user output
1568	- Forensic/debugging information
1569	- Sub-operations within a larger step
1570	
1571	### ✅ Code Display
1572	
1573	```bash
1574	buc_code "command-to-run --with-args"       # Cyan, for showing commands
1575	```
1576	
1577	### ❌ Anti-Patterns
1578	
1579	```bash
1580	# Never use raw echo for user messages
1581	echo "Starting operation..."
1582	
1583	# Never use comments for runtime information
1584	# Processing items    ❌
1585	buc_log_args "Processing items"  ✅
1586	
1587	# Never mix stdout and stderr purposes
1588	echo "Result: ${z_value}"          # If this is return data
1589	echo "Progress update..." >&2      # If this is progress info
1590	```

1607	## Quick Reference Decision Matrix
1608	
1609	| Situation           | Use This Pattern                                                 |
1610	|---------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
1611	| Module structure    | Always split: implementation + CLI                               |
1612	| Need return value   | Check error handling decision tree                               |
1613	| Simple validation   | `test ... \|\| buc_die`                                          |
1614	| Config validation   | Regime archetype: enrollment → enforce → report                  |
1615	| User feedback       | `buc_step` for major milestones, `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail    |
1616	| Runtime information | `buc_log_«source»` for details, `buc_step` for milestones, never comments |
1617	| Show commands       | `buc_code`                                                       |
1618	| Non-fatal issue     | `buc_warn`                                                       |
1619	| Fatal error         | `buc_die`                                                        |
1620	| Success message     | `buc_success`                                                    |
1621	| Variable expansion  | Always `"${var}"`                                                |
1622	| Control flow after test | `test ... \|\| break/continue/return` (never `&&`)           |
1623	| Conditional tests   | `test` not `[[ ]]`                                               |
1624	| Pattern matching    | `[[ var =~ pattern ]]` only                                      |
1625	| Secret extraction   | `_capture` function, never temp files                            |
1626	| Roll population + return | `_enroll` function (kindle only), `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars |
1627	| Roll read access    | `_recite` function                                               |
1628	| True/false check    | `_predicate` function                                            |
1629	| Runtime information | `buc_log_«source»`/`buc_step`, never comments                             |
1630	| File paths          | Kindle constants — defined in kindle only                        |
1631	| File reading        | Single-line `$(<file)` with validation                           |
1632	| Function structure  | See Function Patterns tables                                     |
1633	| Error handling      | See Error Handling Decision Tree                                 |
1634	| Forensic detail (vars)  | `buc_log_args "message ${z_var}"` |
1635	| Forensic detail (output)| `command \| buc_log_pipe`         |

1638	
1639	## Module Maturity Checklist
1640	
1641	### Module Structure
1642	- [ ] Implementation file «prefix»_«name».sh exists
1643	- [ ] CLI file «prefix»_cli.sh exists (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
1644	- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`)
1645	- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
1646	- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function)
1647	- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other deps sourced in furnish
1648	- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
1649	- [ ] `_cli.sh` files are executable; all other `*.sh` files in the module directory are not
1650	
1651	### Required Functions
1652	- [ ] `z«prefix»_kindle` - first line: `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die`
1653	- [ ] `z«prefix»_sentinel` - first line: `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die`
1654	- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` (CLI only) - documents env vars, sources all deps, calls kindle; gates with `buc_doc_env_done`
1655	- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` (regime archetype only) - `buv_vet` + custom format checks after kindle
1656	- [ ] All public functions start with sentinel check
1657	- [ ] All internal helpers prefixed with `z«prefix»_`
1658	
1659	### Variable Management
1660	- [ ] All kindle constants (internal `Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING` and public `«PREFIX»_SCREAMING`) defined exclusively in kindle with `readonly`
1661	- [ ] No kindle constant assignments outside kindle function
1662	- [ ] Mutable kindle state (counters, rolls, builder state) uses lowercase `z_«prefix»_name` — no `readonly`
1663	- [ ] Tinder constants (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) resolve at source time — pure literals or `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` expansion of earlier tinder from the same module; no `$(…)` computation; no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables; placed after `SOURCED` guard
1664	- [ ] All local variables use `z_` prefix
1665	- [ ] All expansions use `"${var}"` pattern (braced, quoted)
1666	- [ ] Parameters use `"${1:-}"` pattern for defensive programming
1667	- [ ] Module state variable `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` is the last statement in kindle
1668	- [ ] No bare `$var` or unbraced `"$var"` expansions
1669	- [ ] No tilde in quoted strings — use `${HOME}` instead
1670	- [ ] No `local -i` — use plain local with explicit validation
1671	- [ ] No raw `eval` for value assignment — use `printf -v` after name validation; `${!name}` for reading
1672	
1673	### Error Handling
1674	- [ ] Every command that can fail has `|| buc_die` (`|| buc_warn` only with a human-authored comment granting permission and explaining why non-fatal is safe)
1675	- [ ] `_predicate` functions return 0/1, never die, no output
1676	- [ ] `_capture` functions output once at end or exit 1, no stderr
1677	- [ ] `_enroll` functions set `z_«funcname»_«retval»` return vars, never echo; callers never use `$()` (when applicable)
1678	- [ ] `_enroll` functions called only within kindle (when applicable)
1679	- [ ] `_recite` functions never mutate roll arrays (when applicable)
1680	- [ ] Roll arrays use `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` naming convention (when applicable)
1681	- [ ] Two-line pattern for capturing: `z_var=$(func_capture) || buc_die`
1682	- [ ] File reads validated: `test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die`
1683	- [ ] Pipelines either: occur inside a `_capture` function, write to temp files with explicit status checks, or explicitly inspect `${PIPESTATUS[@]}`
1684	- [ ] No `test $(command)` — use `grep -q` or `case` for validation
1685	- [ ] Only `_predicate` functions in `if`/`while` conditions — no regular/enroll functions in conditionals

1783	---
1784	
1785	## Test Execution Patterns
1786	
1787	### Principled Subshell Use
1788	
1789	BCG prohibits subshells for error handling (see **Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group**). Test execution is the principled counterpoint: subshells provide **isolation boundaries** that prevent state leakage between fixtures and cases.
1790	
1791	In bash 3.2, there is no module system, no scope isolation, and no cleanup-on-exit guarantee. When fixture A kindles modules and fixture B kindles the same modules, the kindle guards (`Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED`) from fixture A block fixture B. Subshells solve this: all state — kindle guards, sourced functions, variables — dies at the `)` boundary.
1792	
1793	This is discipline encoded as structure.
1794	
1795	### Three-Layer Model
1796	
1797	Test execution uses three layers separated by two subshell boundaries:
1798	
1799	```
1800	Parent Shell (runner layer)
1801	 ├─ Fixture iteration and status tracking
1802	 └─ Reporting (pass/fail/skip counts)
1803	     │
1804	     └─ Fixture Subshell (fixture boundary)
1805	         ├─ _baste — kindle, source, configure
1806	         ├─ Fixture temp dir creation
1807	         └─ Case iteration
1808	             │
1809	             └─ Case Subshell (_tcase boundary)
1810	                 ├─ Per-case BUT_TEMP_DIR and BUTE_BURV_ROOT
1811	                 ├─ Assertions and verification
1812	                 └─ Exit status communicated to fixture
1813	```
1814	
1815	### Vocabulary
1816	
1817	**Fixture boundary** — the fixture isolation boundary:
1818	- Runs in a subshell (state dies at boundary)
1819	- Baste runs inside (visible to cases, dies with fixture)
1820	- Communicates only exit status to the runner
1821	- Kindle guards catch double-kindle within a fixture; subshell boundary prevents cross-fixture contamination
1822	
1823	**`_tcase`** — the case isolation boundary:
1824	- Runs in a subshell within the fixture subshell
1825	- Inherits baste state, cannot mutate sibling state
1826	- Communicates only exit status and stdio
1827	- Each case gets isolated temp dir and BURV root
1828	
1829	**`_baste`** — fixture preparation function (fixture layer):
1830	- Runs inside the fixture subshell
1831	- Kindles modules, sources dependencies, sets configuration
1832	- State visible to all cases within this fixture, dies at fixture boundary
1833	- Registered as third argument to `butr_fixture_enroll`
1834	
1835	### Allowed Operations by Layer
1836	
1837	| Operation | Parent | Fixture | `_tcase` |
1838	|-----------|--------|---------|----------|
1839	| `z«prefix»_kindle` | — | ✅ | — |
1840	| `source` module files | — | ✅ | — |
1841	| Baste configuration | — | ✅ | — |
1842	| Case iteration | — | ✅ | — |
1843	| Assertions (`buto_*_expect_*`) | — | — | ✅ |
1844	| `zbuto_invoke` (capture) | — | — | ✅ |
1845	| Tabtarget invocation | — | — | ✅ |
1846	| Status tracking (pass/fail/skip) | ✅ | — | — |
1847	
1848	### Communication Across Boundaries
1849	
1850	| Boundary | Crosses | Does Not Cross |
1851	|----------|---------|----------------|
1852	| Fixture → parent | Exit status (0=pass, non-zero=fail, 2=skip), stdout/stderr | Variables, kindle state, sourced functions |
1853	| `_tcase` → fixture | Exit status (0=pass, non-zero=fail), stdout/stderr | Variable mutations, BURV roots, temp dirs |
1854	
1855	### Naming Conventions
1856	
1857	| Element | Pattern | Example |
1858	|---------|---------|---------|
1859	| Testbench file | `«prefix»tb_testbench.sh` | `rbtb_testbench.sh` |
1860	| Test case file | `«prefix»tc«xx»_«Name».sh` | `rbtckk_KickTires.sh` |
1861	| Baste function | `z«tb»_«name»_baste` | `zrbtb_ark_baste` |
1862	| Case function | `«tc»_«name»_tcase` | `rbtckk_false_tcase` |
1863	| Fixture enrollment | `butr_fixture_enroll` | — |
1864	| Suite enrollment | `butr_suite_enroll` | Sets suite context |
1865	| Case enrollment | `butr_case_enroll` | — |
1866	
1867	### Compliance Checking
1868	
1869	The naming conventions enable grep-based auditing:
1870	
1871	```bash
1872	# Find all baste functions
1873	grep -rn '_baste' Tools/
1874	
1875	# Find all case functions
1876	grep -rn '_tcase' Tools/
1877	
1878	# Verify enrollment matches naming — baste/case functions should
1879	# appear both in enrollment calls and as function definitions
1880	grep -rn 'butr_fixture_enroll\|butr_case_enroll' Tools/
1881	```
1882	
1883	---
1884	
1885	## Shellcheck Integration
1886	
1887	BCG uses [shellcheck](https://www.shellcheck.net) for static analysis of bash source with a curated suppress list that eliminates false positives caused by BCG's module architecture.
1888	
1889	### Architecture
1890	
1891	| Artifact | Location | Purpose |
1892	|----------|----------|---------|
1893	| `busc_shellcheckrc` | `Tools/buk/` | BCG-structural suppressions with rationale |
1894	| `buq_shellcheck` | `Tools/buk/buq_qualify.sh` | Reusable BUK shellcheck engine; hard-requires shellcheck (`buc_die` when absent) |
1895	| `rbq_qualify_fast` | `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` | Invokes `buq_shellcheck` as part of the `rbw-tq` fast-qualify gate |
1896	
1897	### Suppressed Codes (BCG-structural)
1898	
1899	These codes are false positives caused by BCG's dynamic sourcing, cross-module constants, indirect dispatch, and template-generation patterns:
1900	
1901	| Code | Reason |
1902	|------|--------|
1903	| SC1090 | Can't follow non-constant source — BCG furnish pattern |
1904	| SC1091 | Not following sourced file — same root cause |
1905	| SC2034 | Variable appears unused — cross-module kindle/tinder constants |
1906	| SC2154 | Variable not assigned — inverse of SC2034 |
1907	| SC2155 | Declare and assign separately — BCG blesses single-line `$(<file)` |
1908	| SC2153 | Possible misspelling — cross-module prefix similarity |
1909	| SC2329 | Function never invoked — indirect invocation via source/dispatch |
1910	| SC2016 | Expressions in single quotes — template-generation patterns echo single-quoted strings containing `${}` into generated files (tabtargets, launchers, Dockerfiles) |
1911	| SC2254 | Unquoted case pattern — BCG glob-matching patterns intentionally use unquoted variables in case statements |
1912	| SC2059 | Variable in printf format string — BCG table-formatting patterns use `local -r` format constants shared across header, separator, and data rows |
1913	
1914	### Policy
1915	
1916	1. **No inline directives.** All structural suppressions live in `busc_shellcheckrc`. See **Prohibited Constructs § Inline Shellcheck Directives**.
1917	2. **Genuine findings must be fixed.** If shellcheck flags code that isn't BCG-structural, the code has a real issue.
1918	3. **New structural codes** discovered during adoption should be added to `busc_shellcheckrc` with rationale — never suppressed inline.
1919	
1920	---
1921	
1922	## Fading Memory — Superseded Conventions
1923	
1924	This section documents conventions that have been replaced. When encountering legacy code using these patterns, transform to the current convention. Do not bulk-rename across the codebase — migrate opportunistically when touching affected files.
1925	
1926	### FM-001: _register → _enroll
1927	
1928	**Superseded pattern:**
1929	- Function suffix: `_register`
1930	- Return variables: `z1z_«prefix»_«term»`
1931	- Array naming: no `_roll` suffix convention
1932	- Accessor naming: ad-hoc `_get_*` functions
1933	
1934	**Recognition — legacy code looks like:**
1935	- Functions named `*_register` that populate parallel arrays
1936	- Variables starting with `z1z_` used as return channels
1937	- Parallel arrays without `_roll` suffix (e.g., `zbuz_colophons` instead of `z_buz_colophon_roll`)
1938	- Accessor functions named `*_get_*` without `_recite` suffix
1939	
1940	**Current convention:**
1941	- Function suffix: `_enroll` (kindle-only)
1942	- Return variables: `z_«funcname»_«retval»` (function name embedded verbatim)
1943	- Array naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll`
1944	- Accessor suffix: `_recite` (read-only, never mutates)
1945	
1946	**Known legacy sites:**
1947	- `buz_register` in `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh`
1948	- `butr_register` in `Tools/buk/butr_registry.sh`
1949	
1950	**Migration:** When touching a file that uses the old pattern, transform to new. Enroll calls must move into kindle. Return variables must use `z_«funcname»_«retval»`. Arrays must gain `_roll` suffix using `z_«prefix»_` convention. Accessors must gain `_recite` suffix.
